summary: US Repeater Network — a professional ham radio repeater directory for the United States. Features a fun logo with antenna tower/HT/base station, full RepeaterBook-style database fields, zip code proximity filtering, ~20-30 sample repeaters across 5 states, user submission system, hardcoded passphrase admin dashboard (WendellAdmin2024) for Wendell to approve submissions, and favorites feature for logged-in users via Internet Identity. Now integrating live RepeaterBook API (https://www.repeaterbook.com/api/export.php) for real ham radio repeater data; GMRS repeaters handled via manual user submissions.
architectural_notes: Admin access protected by hardcoded passphrase (not Internet Identity)
Proximity filtering uses built-in zip code coordinate dataset for distance calculations — no GPS or map rendering
User authentication via Internet Identity for favorites and submissions
RepeaterBook API integration calls https://www.repeaterbook.com/api/export.php for live repeater data; no API key required but rate limits must be respected

existing_features.title: Build a professional public-facing repeater directory page as the main landing screen. Display approved repeaters in a searchable, filterable table/card list. Include filter controls for: state (dropdown), city (text input), zip code + radius selector (10/25/50/100 miles). Each repeater entry must display: call sign, frequency, offset, CTCSS/DCS tone, city, state, sponsor, coverage, and a 'View Details' action. Logged-in users see a star/favorite button on each entry.

Create a repeater detail modal or page that shows all fields for a selected repeater: frequency, offset, call sign, sponsor, city, state, zip, CTCSS tone, DCS code, tone mode, coverage description, operational notes, autopatch info, link info, and status. Include a favorite toggle for logged-in users.

Build a user repeater submission form accessible from the main directory page. The form must include all repeater fields (frequency, offset, call sign, sponsor, city, state, zip, CTCSS/DCS tones, coverage, operational notes, autopatch info, link info). Submissions are sent to the backend with pending status. Show a success message confirming the submission is awaiting admin review.

Build an admin dashboard accessible via a protected route (e.g., /admin). Entry requires entering a hardcoded passphrase (decided at implementation time, e.g., 'WendellAdmin2024'). The dashboard lists all pending repeater submissions with full details and Approve / Reject action buttons. Approved repeaters become visible in the public directory immediately. Include a tab or section for viewing all approved repeaters with a delete option.

Integrate Internet Identity login into the app header/navigation. Show a 'Login' button when unauthenticated and the user's principal (abbreviated) with a 'Logout' button when authenticated. Favorites functionality requires login; prompt unauthenticated users to log in when they attempt to favorite a repeater.

Apply a professional, high-energy ham radio / communications theme throughout the app. Use a dark navy/charcoal background with bright amber/orange and green accent colors reminiscent of radio equipment and RF signal meters. Use a bold, technical sans-serif typography. The layout should feel like a professional operations console — clean grid-based directory, prominent search bar, and clear status indicators (active/inactive) with colored badges. Apply this theme consistently across all pages.
Display the generated logo image (antenna-tower-logo.png) prominently in the app header/navigation bar alongside the site name 'US Repeater Network'.

Create a frontend service/utility module that calls the RepeaterBook public API at https://www.repeaterbook.com/api/export.php. The module must support querying by state (using the 'state_id' parameter with 2-letter state abbreviation), by city (using 'city' parameter), and optionally by frequency. It must parse the JSON response and map RepeaterBook fields (Frequency, Offset, Uplink Tone, Downlink Tone, TSQ, Call Sign, Nearest City, State, Use, Operational Status, etc.) into the app's internal Repeater shape so it is compatible with existing RepeaterCard and RepeaterDetailModal components.

Update the Directory Page (frontend/src/pages/DirectoryPage.tsx) to integrate the RepeaterBook API. When a user selects a state or enters a city in the filter bar, the page should query the RepeaterBook API for live repeater data in addition to (or as a toggle alongside) the locally stored repeaters. Display a loading indicator while fetching. Show RepeaterBook results in the same RepeaterCard grid. Include a visible source badge or label on each card indicating whether the data is from 'RepeaterBook' (live) or 'Local' (user-submitted). Do not remove the existing local repeater display — both sources should coexist.

Ensure the RepeaterDetail modal (frontend/src/components/RepeaterDetailModal.tsx) works correctly for both local repeaters and RepeaterBook-sourced repeaters. RepeaterBook results may not have all fields (e.g., no repeaterId for favorites). For RepeaterBook entries, gracefully handle missing fields by showing 'N/A' and disable the favorite button with a tooltip explaining that favoriting is only available for locally submitted repeaters.

Add a prominent informational banner or section on the Directory Page stating that live ham radio repeater data is sourced from RepeaterBook.com, and that GMRS repeaters can be submitted manually via the submission form. Include a link to RepeaterBook.com (https://www.repeaterbook.com) that opens in a new tab. Style the banner consistent with the existing ham-radio dark theme.

Create a frontend service/utility module at frontend/src/services/repeaterBookApi.ts that calls the RepeaterBook public API at https://www.repeaterbook.com/api/export.php. The module must support querying by state (using the 'state_id' parameter with 2-letter state abbreviation), by city (using the 'city' parameter), and optionally by frequency. It must parse the JSON response and map RepeaterBook fields (Frequency, Offset, Uplink Tone, Downlink Tone, TSQ, Call Sign, Nearest City, State, Use, Operational Status, etc.) into the app's internal Repeater shape so it is compatible with existing RepeaterCard and RepeaterDetailModal components. Mark these entries with a source field set to 'repeaterbook' to distinguish them from locally submitted repeaters.

Update the Directory Page (frontend/src/pages/DirectoryPage.tsx) to integrate the RepeaterBook API. When a user selects a state or enters a city in the filter bar, query the RepeaterBook API for live repeater data in addition to the locally stored repeaters. Display a loading indicator while fetching. Show RepeaterBook results in the same RepeaterCard grid alongside local results. Include a visible source badge or label on each card indicating whether the data is from 'RepeaterBook' (live) or 'Local' (user-submitted). Both sources must coexist — do not remove or hide existing local repeater display.

Ensure the RepeaterDetailModal (frontend/src/components/RepeaterDetailModal.tsx) works correctly for both local repeaters and RepeaterBook-sourced repeaters. For RepeaterBook entries that lack certain fields, display 'N/A' for missing values. Disable the favorite button for RepeaterBook entries and show a tooltip explaining that favoriting is only available for locally submitted repeaters.

Add a prominent informational banner on the Directory Page (frontend/src/pages/DirectoryPage.tsx) stating that live ham radio repeater data is sourced from RepeaterBook.com, and that GMRS repeaters can be submitted manually via the submission form. Include a clickable link to https://www.repeaterbook.com that opens in a new tab. Style the banner consistent with the existing dark ham-radio theme using amber/orange accent colors.

new_feature_requests.summary: Fun logo with antenna tower, HT, and base station
Full RepeaterBook-style fields (frequency, offset, call sign, sponsor, CTCSS/DCS tones, coverage, operational notes, autopatch info)
Search by state, city, and zip code with 10/25/50/100 mile proximity filtering using built-in zip code coordinate dataset
20-30 sample repeaters pre-loaded across 5 states to simulate a real directory
User submission system for adding/updating repeaters
Admin dashboard protected by hardcoded passphrase for Wendell to review and approve submissions
Favorite repeaters feature for logged-in users via Internet Identity

Create a Motoko backend actor that stores a complete repeater data model with all RepeaterBook-style fields: frequency (Float), offset (Float), call sign (Text), sponsor (Text), city (Text), state (Text), zip code (Text), CTCSS tone (Text), DCS code (Text), tone mode (Text), coverage description (Text), operational notes (Text), autopatch info (Text), link info (Text), status (active/inactive), submission status (pending/approved/rejected), submitted by (Text), and a unique ID. Provide CRUD operations: add repeater (creates with pending status), approve/reject repeater (admin only, passphrase-protected), get approved repeaters, get pending repeaters (admin), update repeater, delete repeater (admin).

Pre-load approximately 20–30 sample repeaters spread across 5 US states (e.g., California, Texas, Florida, New York, and Illinois) as initial seed data in the backend. Each record must be fully populated with realistic ham radio repeater values including frequency, offset, call sign, sponsor, tones, coverage, and operational notes, and must be set to approved status.

Implement zip code proximity filtering in the backend using a built-in zip code coordinate dataset (latitude/longitude lookup table embedded in the Motoko actor). Provide a search function that accepts a zip code and a radius (10, 25, 50, or 100 miles) and returns all approved repeaters whose zip code falls within that radius using the Haversine formula for distance calculation.

Implement a favorites system in the backend. Authenticated users (identified by their Internet Identity principal) can add or remove approved repeaters from their favorites list. Provide getFavorites(principal), addFavorite(principal, repeaterId), and removeFavorite(principal, repeaterId) functions.

Integrate RepeaterBook API (https://www.repeaterbook.com/api/export.php) to fetch live real ham radio repeater data by state, city, or frequency when users search, replacing or supplementing sample data. Keep existing submission system for GMRS repeaters.
